title: New Car

jokes.text: What are these sad print-outs?
jokes.setup: Johnny has prepared a formal proposal with printed materials for the family
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses his earnest effort with a single withering adjective — 'sad'
jokes.explanation: A tight, character-perfect opener. Moira's casual condescension is her signature mode, and 'sad print-outs' perfectly captures how she processes the mundane world through an aesthetic lens of disappointment. Economy is excellent — two words do heavy lifting.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh David, these are positively tragic. Your father's little research materials? They're so... pedestrian. I mean, really, who curates their own existential crisis with such dreary organization? It's the saddest thing I've seen since that time I wore last season's Valentino to the club.

jokes.text: Are the police auctioning off crime scene vehicles?
jokes.setup: Johnny says he has a proposal for the family; Moira asks what kind
jokes.punchline: Her mind immediately leaps to the most dramatic possible interpretation of budget car shopping
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp character joke: Moira's logic is internally consistent (crime scene vehicles = cheap cars = what poor people buy), and the specificity of 'crime scene vehicles' elevates it above a simple 'are we buying something gross' beat. Very Moira — theatrical, specific, and delivered with total sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: These prices are far too reasonable. These must be seized vehicles from crime scenes. Blood money cars, if you will.

jokes.text: I would rather drive a scooter. And we all know how I feel about scooters, those... stupid helmets.
jokes.setup: Johnny shows car examples; David says he'd rather have a scooter
jokes.punchline: His reason for disliking scooters isn't the vehicle itself — it's the helmet's aesthetic offense
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose: his objection is entirely about appearance and personal brand. The trailing '...stupid helmets' delivered with exhausted contempt is very characteristic. The ellipsis implies he searched for a more articulate objection and gave up.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, absolutely not. These are all completely unacceptable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you need transportation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would rather walk. Actually, I would rather not go places at all.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, what about a scooter?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A scooter? Have you seen the helmets? They're shaped like a teardrop. I'm not going through life looking like a depressed water droplet.

jokes.text: Stevie... has a car.
jokes.setup: Johnny is making the case that everyone has a car — Roland, Bob with three cars
jokes.punchline: The pause before 'Stevie' and the deflated delivery signals that even Johnny knows citing the motel desk clerk as his benchmark is humiliating
jokes.explanation: The comedic value is entirely in the implied pause and the escalating sadness of the list: Roland (fine), Bob with THREE cars (oof), and then... Stevie. The descent from wealthy peer to motel clerk as the final benchmark captures the Rose family's fallen status perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Are there boats for sale online?

jokes.text: Does this site have boats?
jokes.setup: The family is arguing about the used car website and their $4,000 budget
jokes.punchline: Moira, completely ignoring the discussion, asks about boats — revealing she is not operating in the same financial reality
jokes.explanation: Four words and it's devastating. Moira has checked out of the practical conversation entirely and is browsing for boats. The non-sequitur is perfectly timed and perfectly in character — she is constitutionally incapable of engaging with budget constraints.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a spreadsheet. I organized everything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're welcome.

jokes.text: Is that a spreadsheet? — Yes, yes, it is.
jokes.setup: Johnny has prepared a formal pitch with a spreadsheet
jokes.punchline: Moira's incredulous tone treats 'spreadsheet' like a foreign artifact, and Johnny's slightly defeated confirmation implies he knows this was a doomed effort
jokes.explanation: Functional beat that lands on the Rose family's relationship to practical planning. Not especially original but well-played — Johnny's 'Yes, yes, it is' carries the weary pride of a man who knows he's already lost.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, we need to establish some ground rules about who uses the car and when.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine. You can have Tuesdays and Thursdays.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We don't even own a car yet.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, when we do, you'll have Tuesdays and Thursdays. I've already blocked them off in my calendar.

jokes.text: Okay, well, I'm gonna need the car on Friday nights. — Absolutely not. — Meaning that I would get it Saturday.
jokes.setup: They don't even have the car yet; Johnny is mid-pitch
jokes.punchline: David immediately negotiates for car access, and when blocked, pivots to Saturday — treating 'no' as a counter-offer rather than a refusal
jokes.explanation: The three-beat rhythm is well-constructed: request, rejection, immediate negotiation for adjacent terms. 'We don't even have the car yet!' is Johnny's exasperated button. David's logic — treating 'no' as an opening position — is very true to his entitled-but-resourceful character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your automotive history is a fever dream of poor decisions. I can still see you in that canary yellow Hummer, one hand on the wheel, the other adjusting your sunglasses like you were Vin Diesel in Fast & Furious.

jokes.text: This is not your best pitch, darling. — I haven't even started the pitch, Moira!
jokes.setup: The family has completely derailed Johnny's planned presentation before he could begin
jokes.punchline: Moira critiques the pitch he hasn't given yet; Johnny points out this fact with building frustration
jokes.explanation: Good structural joke: Moira's critique is delivered with the same authority she'd use if she'd watched a full presentation, and the punchline is Johnny pointing out the fundamental absurdity. The escalating chaos of the pre-pitch demolishes the premise neatly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A car is a reflection of one's self. What does yours say about you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That I have crippling debt?

jokes.text: Okay, you know what?! I'm buying a car! End of story. — Where are you going? — Here.
jokes.setup: Johnny declares he's buying a car and storms off dramatically
jokes.punchline: He immediately sits back down — there's nowhere to storm to in the tiny motel room
jokes.explanation: The single-word punchline 'Here' is perfect economy. The visual gag of a man dramatically storming nowhere because his home is one tiny room captures the Rose family's diminished circumstances better than any expository dialogue could. The physical constraint becomes the joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you're wearing a ball gown to look at used cars. You look like you're about to auction yourself off.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I like to dress for the occasion.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The occasion is a 2008 Honda Civic, not a gala.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, I dress how I dress.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know, and it's exactly like— wait.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm wearing a three-thousand-dollar jacket to a car dealership.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We're terrible people.

jokes.text: sometimes your choices are less suited to a family's needs, and more suited to a pony-tailed Lothario, cruising down the Monte Carlo coast. Or like um, Vin Diesel.
jokes.setup: Moira is diplomatically explaining that Johnny's taste in cars isn't family-appropriate
jokes.punchline: Her escalating poetic description of his car-buying alter-ego culminates in 'Or like um, Vin Diesel' — a jarring, very specific and very un-Moira cultural reference delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the opening act. The setup is Moira at her most florid — 'pony-tailed Lothario, cruising down the Monte Carlo coast' — grandiose, literary, and completely consistent with her theatrical register. Then 'Or like um, Vin Diesel.' The gear-shift to a specific, contemporary, utterly non-Moira reference, delivered with the same earnest confidence, is a genuine subversion. The 'um' mid-sentence sells that she actually searched her mental database for someone Johnny would relate to and landed there. High quotability, strong character integration, earns its laughs.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not just going to wear a suit.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're a moving target for my expensive clothing.

jokes.text: It's called style, people.
jokes.setup: Moira has just compared Johnny's car preferences to a Vin Diesel movie
jokes.punchline: Johnny's dignified, generic defense — 'It's called style, people' — directed at his own family, as if they're an audience who doesn't get it
jokes.explanation: Functional button to the Vin Diesel joke. Johnny's dignity-preserving non-answer works because of his delivery — it's the response of a man who refuses to be defeated — but it's not a remarkable joke on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No cufflinks.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, the disenfranchisement of it all.

jokes.text: Not dressed like that, you can't. — You bought me this dress, David. — I know! And it cost twice as much as the car you wanna buy.
jokes.setup: Moira says she can find a vintage car on budget; David says she can't pull it off dressed the way she is
jokes.punchline: When Moira reveals David bought her the dress, he pivots instantly to using its price as evidence of the absurdity — the dress cost more than their car budget
jokes.explanation: Three-beat joke with a good turn: David's criticism backfires when his own purchase is implicated, but rather than backing down he uses it to make a different, sharper point about their financial situation. The Rose family's warped relationship with money is on full display.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Sorry I'm late. There was just so much heavy foot traffic on the way here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, it's Schitt's Creek. There are like twelve people who live here.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, they were all walking.

jokes.text: No used car salesman is gonna cut you a deal dressed in archival designer silk.
jokes.setup: The family is debating how to dress to appear relatable at the car lot
jokes.punchline: David's fashion-specific terminology ('archival designer silk') while giving practical consumer advice creates an absurd register mismatch
jokes.explanation: The joke works because David is simultaneously the most fashion-conscious person in the room and the one giving practical class-performance advice. He knows exactly how their wealth reads to the outside world even as he inhabits it. 'Archival designer silk' is perfectly specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, what happened to you? You look like you got in a fight.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I fell off my bike.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh wow, okay. Well, you look really cool now. Like, legitimately tough. I'm kind of into it.

jokes.text: Yeah, says the moving target in the Bespoke suit.
jokes.setup: David has criticized Moira's appearance for the car lot; Johnny agrees with David
jokes.punchline: Moira fires back at David's hypocrisy — he's also overdressed — calling him a 'moving target'
jokes.explanation: Solid comeback. 'Moving target' is an evocative image — David's elaborate, attention-grabbing style makes him conspicuous. The Bespoke suit reference reinforces the family's wealth-signaling problem. Not the sharpest line but well-placed in the volley.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, what happened to you? Did you get attacked by a bear? Were you in a car accident? Did someone try to kidnap you?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, I just fell down the stairs at home.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's... disappointingly mundane.

jokes.text: I've got a ball cap I can put on.
jokes.setup: Johnny insists he can dress down for the car lot while wearing a bespoke suit
jokes.punchline: His solution to appearing working-class is... a ball cap on top of the suit
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective. The image of John Rose in a bespoke suit with a ball cap trying to pass as regular is inherently funny. 'I've got a ball cap I can put on' delivered with earnest confidence is very Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are you seeing someone?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: What? No.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're being so secretive. What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I've been setting aside money for my RRSP.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: ...Ted, that's not a secret. That's just sad.

jokes.text: to mimic the trappings of a workaday woman. And if you'll notice, David, no cufflinks.
jokes.setup: Moira has assembled an 'appropriate look' for the car lot to appear working-class
jokes.punchline: Her idea of dressing down is removing cufflinks — she presents this as a radical sacrifice and expects credit
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Moira jokes. 'Mimic the trappings of a workaday woman' is delicious — the anthropological distance in that phrase, as if working women are a species to be studied. And the triumphant reveal of 'no cufflinks' as her concession to poverty is perfectly calibrated. She is absolutely sincere, which makes it devastating. High character integration — only Moira Rose would say this.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I've been taking something to help me focus at work.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's horse tranquilizer.

jokes.text: Wow! I am blinded by the disenfranchisement.
jokes.setup: Moira has revealed her 'dressed down' look, which still reads as extremely wealthy
jokes.punchline: David's sarcastic mock-awe — 'blinded by the disenfranchisement' — is a perfect satirical phrase
jokes.explanation: This is the best individual joke in the episode. 'I am blinded by the disenfranchisement' is a genuinely brilliant phrase — sarcastic but using language that perfectly mirrors Moira's own register, which makes it both a comeback and a parody. 'Blinded by the disenfranchisement' is instantly quotable, deeply specific to this family's situation, and could only be delivered by David Rose, who is fluent in irony and class critique simultaneously. The economy is near-perfect: seven words carry enormous comedic weight. This would land as a big laugh in any room.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Why is it so dark in here?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Is someone dead, or are you going through a mid-life Goth phase?

jokes.text: I wouldn't be surprised if they just gave you the car.
jokes.setup: Moira has unveiled her costume as a 'workaday woman' which is obviously still extravagant
jokes.punchline: Johnny's dry observation that the look is so pathetically convincing as poverty that someone might give her the car — delivered sincerely, as if it might actually work
jokes.explanation: Good button on the costuming sequence. Johnny's deadpan vote of confidence is actually foreshadowing — Moira's performance does work on the car salesman. Gets a solid laugh as a closer to the scene.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, he's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, the lighting in here is still terrible.

jokes.text: Who is this cool cat, and what did he do with Ted?
jokes.setup: Ted arrives to work looking noticeably different — apparently disheveled or with a visible injury
jokes.punchline: Alexis pretends not to recognize him because his appearance is so altered; the friendly phrasing 'cool cat' is ironic if he looks rough
jokes.explanation: Functional greeting joke that sets up the Ted subplot. 'Cool cat' is playfully retro and slightly Alexis-coded. Works as a scene-opener but not especially sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, are you joking?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I'm not joking. I'll be dead by 52.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What? How do you know that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My mother read it in a magazine.

jokes.text: Did you get in a bar fight?
jokes.setup: Ted has arrived with an obvious visible injury he doesn't want to discuss
jokes.punchline: Alexis's immediate guess — bar fight — reveals what category of explanation she finds most plausible for Ted, which is absurd given his characteristically wholesome demeanor
jokes.explanation: The humor comes from the mismatch between the very un-bar-fight-ish Ted and Alexis's mental model. Decent but light — the real payoff comes later with the goat reveal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, please. I've been called worse things by a Pottery Barn employee.

jokes.text: You owe someone money. — Alexis, I pay off my credit card bills two weeks in advance, what do you think?
jokes.setup: Alexis is guessing what Ted's secret is
jokes.punchline: Ted's indignant response — not denying having a secret, but defending his credit score — is a very specific, very Ted form of protest
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp character joke: Ted is so straightlaced that his instinct when accused of owing money is to cite his credit habits. 'Two weeks in advance' is the perfect detail — not just on time, but aggressively on time. Very Ted. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I liked her because she was a mess.

jokes.text: Okay, fine. I know that they are for the animals, but I find that when I get tired, just one or two kind of help me focus.
jokes.setup: Alexis pushes Ted to reveal his secret; the audience expects something scandalous
jokes.punchline: Ted casually reveals he takes medication meant for animals himself — framed as a perfectly reasonable self-care choice
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection: the audience — and Alexis — expects something romantic or embarrassing, and gets something weirder and darker. The casual framing ('just one or two kind of help me focus') makes it more disturbing and funny than an outright admission would. Good economy of reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I could... help you, if you want.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, don't strain yourself with enthusiasm.

jokes.text: What are you talking about?! Anyway, um, secret or no weird secret, we've gotta cover that thing up. I hope you're not into contouring, 'cause it's not really my thing.
jokes.setup: Ted has just revealed he takes animal medication; Alexis immediately pivots to his injury
jokes.punchline: Alexis processes the animal medication revelation in exactly zero seconds and moves straight to beauty advice — her priorities are immovable
jokes.explanation: Alexis's complete non-reaction to the animal drug confession — followed immediately by makeup advice — is very true to her character. The 'contouring' pivot is funny because it treats the serious revelation as a non-event, which tells us everything about Alexis's priorities.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you so much for offering, but I don't think you can handle this. It's very depressing.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, I literally work here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, this is different.

jokes.text: So the low energy bulbs you installed in the bathroom, I'm finding the light to be very harsh, and um... did someone die, or are we going through like, a mid-life Goth phase?
jokes.setup: Stevie enters the room where David is clearly in a dark mood
jokes.punchline: 'Did someone die, or are we going through like, a mid-life Goth phase?' — Stevie deadpans two wildly different explanations with equal weight
jokes.explanation: The construction is good: the light bulb complaint is a mundane entry, and then 'did someone die or mid-life Goth phase' escalates into a binary with two very different stakes. The fact that someone DID die makes this retroactively funny on rewatch. Strong Stevie deadpan — she gives both options equal probability.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm joking!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, I'm not joking.

jokes.text: Someone died. — Okay, I'm just finding the bulbs to make my skin look very jaundice-y.
jokes.setup: Stevie has asked if someone died, half-joking
jokes.punchline: David confirms someone died; Stevie's immediate response is to continue complaining about the lighting
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent cringe comedy. David drops 'someone died' and Stevie — mid-sentence — cannot stop herself from continuing her complaint about looking jaundice-y. It's a beautifully timed social failure, and her inability to adjust her register is deeply true to the character. The word 'jaundice-y' is also physically funny. This beat works because Stevie is always deadpan, and this is deadpan taken to its logical extreme.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Strip cribbage?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God it's not Friday.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, we could just pretend it's Friday.

jokes.text: Okay, I can't tell if we're still joking... or not? — I'm not. — Okay, and now I can't tell whether you're playing into the joke, or whether um... you're telling the truth. — She was 78. — So you're not joking.
jokes.setup: After the jaundice comment, Stevie tries to calibrate whether the death is real
jokes.punchline: The multi-beat confusion loop — neither character can establish what register they're in — culminates in David's utterly flat '78' as evidence, and Stevie's deadpan 'So you're not joking'
jokes.explanation: The extended confusion loop is very well-constructed. Stevie's inability to read the situation, and David's unhelpful responses, creates genuine sustained awkwardness that's funny on every beat. 'She was 78' as the sole clarifying data point is perfectly delivered — David offers this as if it settles everything. Very much in the cringe comedy tradition of characters talking past each other.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I need to look poor so they don't think I have money to spend.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: But you don't have money to spend.

jokes.text: Why, you didn't kill her.
jokes.setup: Stevie apologizes for everything she's said since coming in
jokes.punchline: David's response is darkly matter-of-fact — not 'don't worry about it' but a technical exoneration
jokes.explanation: Five words, perfectly constructed. Instead of the conventional 'don't worry about it,' David issues a legal-style exoneration: the only reason you don't need to apologize is that you didn't cause the death. The implication is that if she had, apologies would be warranted. Economy is near-perfect and it's very David.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh! You want to look like you don't have any money. Well, you've come to the right place, my friend. I've been dressing like this for years.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, I just want something more casual.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Casual poverty. I get it. You want people to think you're broke.

jokes.text: I mean, she was a mess, but I liked her. That's probably why I liked her.
jokes.setup: David explains his relationship with his deceased aunt
jokes.punchline: He instantly self-diagnoses: I liked her BECAUSE she was a mess — said without irony, as complete self-awareness
jokes.explanation: The self-correction into self-awareness is good writing. David's admission that he's drawn to chaotic people is both funny and characterologically revealing. The two-beat rhythm — 'but I liked her / that's probably why I liked her' — lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You want to look hip? I got PVC or Hawaiian shirt. Those are your options.

jokes.text: Stevie's awkward pause before offering help, with clear visible reluctance
jokes.setup: David mentions all the funeral arrangements have fallen to him; Stevie has an opening to offer help
jokes.punchline: The transcript shows '[05:22] Oh. Um, well if you... need hel...' — Stevie visibly cannot complete the offer of help, trailing off for a full 12 seconds before David prompts her
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in reaction-beat comedy. The [05:34] timestamp shows 12 seconds elapsed before David has to demand she finish the sentence. Stevie's instinct to help is real, but her instinct NOT to offer is stronger, and watching her physically fail to complete the sentence is pure character comedy. Perfectly executed non-verbal bit.

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: So we've had a bit of a situation. We've misplaced the ashes.

jokes.text: I'd like you to finish that sentence.
jokes.setup: Stevie has been unable to complete her offer of help for 12 seconds
jokes.punchline: David calmly requests she complete the sentence, treating the failure as a logistical problem to solve
jokes.explanation: Perfect button to the awkward silence bit. David's polite, precise request — 'I'd like you to finish that sentence' — is funnier than any exasperated reaction would be. The formality of the phrasing is very David.

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: Well, you see, we had a bit of a... organizational issue in our records.

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: It appears your mother's ashes may have been... misfiled.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Misfiled? They're ashes. How do you misfile ashes?

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: Well, that's the thing. We've discovered that several of our labeling systems from that period were, shall we say, not up to standard.

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: Your mother's urn may be... any of these twelve urns.

jokes.text: I don't think you could handle it. This whole thing is super depressing.
jokes.setup: Stevie reluctantly offers to help; David says he appreciates her 'reluctant offer'
jokes.punchline: Rather than accepting help graciously, David questions Stevie's emotional capacity — and the reason is that it's 'super depressing,' which is also self-descriptive of David
jokes.explanation: The irony that David — who is himself barely handling it — dismisses Stevie as unable to handle the depression is slightly self-aware. 'Super depressing' as his criteria for gatekeeping friendship is very David Rose.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My father's business practices were somewhat questionable. There was fraud, embezzlement, and... well, let's just say there was gun-play.

jokes.text: That is what um, friends say to each other, right?
jokes.setup: Stevie finally offers help and says 'that is what friends say to each other'
jokes.punchline: She immediately checks herself — 'right?' — as if she's not sure she's using the friendship script correctly
jokes.explanation: This is very touching AND funny — Stevie is so unused to emotional expression that she has to verify she's performing friendship correctly. The 'um' and 'right?' are perfectly placed. Strong character integration — Stevie's emotional guardedness is one of her defining traits.

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: We've located your father's ashes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh thank God. Where were they?

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: In the pasta cabinet.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The pasta cabinet?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's very on-brand for him.

jokes.text: Yes, it is.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if offering help is what friends say to each other
jokes.punchline: David's simple, gentle confirmation — 'Yes, it is' — lands differently than a bigger response would; the restraint is the joke
jokes.explanation: Three words. The economy is extraordinary — David simply confirms, and the warmth underneath the deadpan registers. It's a moment that's funny AND quietly moving, which is Schitt's Creek's signature. On rewatch, knowing these characters' full arc, this hits harder.

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: Do you have any cream or whitener for the coffee?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Um, I think we have some... I'm going to go check.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no. That's not... that's not whitener.

jokes.text: so I'm just gonna change into my funeral blacks.
jokes.setup: Stevie agrees to help with the funeral arrangements
jokes.punchline: She immediately and practically announces she has 'funeral blacks' ready to go — implying she keeps a dedicated funeral wardrobe
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'funeral blacks' (plural, implying a wardrobe category) is very Stevie. It's deadpan delivery of an oddly morbid preparedness. Works as a quiet button on the emotional scene.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. Look at you. A rough and rugged country cousin.

jokes.text: Oh, sorry, we don't want any. — I'm just joking. Come on in! — No, I'm not!
jokes.setup: Johnny arrives at Roland and Jocelyn's door
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn rejects him, Roland invites him in, Jocelyn immediately recants — revealing the couple's rapid-fire marital dynamic
jokes.explanation: The three-beat sequence works as a quick character sketch of the Schitts' marriage. Jocelyn's honest 'no, I'm not' after Roland overrules her is funny precisely because she doesn't pretend otherwise. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, what are you wearing?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a Valentino.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Valentino? Since when do you shop at Valentino?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Since always, darling. This is a custom piece by Valentino Garavani's lesser-known cousin, Sergio Valentino. He designs exclusively for me out of a studio in Montauk.

jokes.text: That's right, and Fridays, it's strip crib.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn invites Johnny to join their daily cribbage game
jokes.punchline: Roland reveals Friday is strip cribbage — an image nobody asked for
jokes.explanation: Roland's casual delivery of strip crib as a Tuesday-Friday distinction, as if that's a normal schedule, is very character-consistent. The joke is in the normalcy of his tone. 'Strip crib' as a compound phrase is inherently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but I think the key difference is that your car had a Jamaican flag bumper sticker on it.

jokes.text: Well, thank God it's not Friday. — Well, we can pretend.
jokes.setup: Roland reveals Fridays are strip cribbage
jokes.punchline: Johnny is relieved it's not Friday; Roland immediately offers to pretend it is
jokes.explanation: Classic two-line escalation. Johnny's relieved 'thank God' followed by Roland's cheerful 'we can pretend' is a very efficient joke that tells you everything about Roland's complete lack of social awareness. The rhythm is clean.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're just simple people trying to make ends meet.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, we subsist on a rather austere diet of legumes and the occasional poultry.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: ...Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What? I'm being poor.

jokes.text: But you don't have any money. — Oh, I know I don't have any money, but I need to look like I don't have money.
jokes.setup: Johnny explains he needs to dress down to look like he doesn't have money at the car lot
jokes.punchline: Roland points out the obvious: he actually doesn't have money. Johnny's response — 'I know, but I need to LOOK like I don't have money' — treats the actual reality as different from the performance
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's sharpest jokes. Johnny's logic is internally impeccable — he's not poor enough LOOKING to get a deal despite actually being poor — and Roland's cut-to observation exposes the absurdity. The line 'I know I don't have any money, but I need to look like I don't have money' is almost philosophical in its paradox. It captures the Rose family's situation perfectly: they are poor but haven't internalized it. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, my 'usband is a tennis pro at the public resort now. 'E's really quite talented with the racket, you know. Very 'ands-on with 'is technique.

jokes.text: Ohhh, okay. Here's what I'm hearing you say, what you're saying is you wanna dress like me, because I look like I don't have any money!
jokes.setup: Johnny has explained he needs to look like he doesn't have money
jokes.punchline: Roland realizes — with delight — that Johnny is asking to dress like Roland because Roland looks broke
jokes.explanation: Roland's dawning, enthusiastic comprehension is perfectly played. He's not offended — he's proud. His identity as the working-class guy is something he wears with zero shame, and the reversal (the rich man needing to perform poverty by borrowing from him) is well-constructed. The slow build of 'Ohhh, okay. Here's what I'm hearing...' is very Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We may not have a roof over our heads, but we do have our dignity and our diction.

jokes.text: I think I misspoke.
jokes.setup: Roland has triumphantly concluded that Johnny wants to dress like him because he looks poor
jokes.punchline: Johnny's understated retreat — 'I think I misspoke' — is a dignity-preserving non-denial
jokes.explanation: Four words that perfectly capture Johnny's dignified retreat. He doesn't argue Roland is wrong — just claims he said it wrong. The economy is excellent and the character-specific pride is intact.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We are living in absolute penury. I mean, we're practically 'omeless.

jokes.text: What do you want, uh PVC, or a Hawaiian shirt?
jokes.setup: Roland enthusiastically agrees to help Johnny find 'hip' clothes
jokes.punchline: His two options for 'hip' clothing are PVC and a Hawaiian shirt — a wardrobe that exists in its own dimension
jokes.explanation: The binary choice of PVC vs. Hawaiian shirt is deeply Roland. Both are equally wrong for the situation, but Roland presents them as a complete menu of hip options. The specificity of PVC is what elevates this above a simple 'he has bad taste' joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's a complicated question. You see, I was actually kidnapped by Russian mobsters when I was just a baby.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They raised me in the Urals until I was eight years old, at which point Interpol finally tracked me down.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My real parents had been searching for me the entire time, of course. They were minor European nobility.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So technically, I'm from the Urals, but spiritually, I'm from the Cotswolds.

jokes.text: That side of my family has a bit of a reputation for conflict. And philandering. And fraud. And gun-play.
jokes.setup: The family inventory has revealed a string of imprisoned and deceased relatives
jokes.punchline: David summarizes the family's issues in an escalating list that culminates in 'gun-play' — delivered as if this is a minor footnote
jokes.explanation: 'Gun-play' is the perfect escalation button. The list — conflict, philandering, fraud — is a normal rich-family scandal catalogue, and then 'gun-play' arrives as if it's equivalent in weight to the others. The word 'gun-play' itself is an archaic, almost genteel euphemism that makes it funnier. David's flat delivery of all four as character traits he's mildly embarrassed by, not horrified by, is great character work.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman
dialogue.line: But if they were twins, wouldn't they have been born at the same time?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Not if one of them was born in the Bratva compound in Belarus. They had a very different calendar system. He came out at 3 o'clock, she came out at 3:15. Completely different times.

jokes.text: We'll just get this one, I guess. — Well, that's the shipping tube we pack the urns in.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie survey the urn options and David picks one
jokes.punchline: He has selected the cardboard shipping tube, not an urn — which is a visual and situational humiliation
jokes.explanation: Classic visual gag delivered through dialogue. David's resigned 'I guess' before the correction is perfect — he's so defeated by the funeral home chaos that he's willing to accept a shipping tube as an urn. The correction is the punchline. Well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the kidnapping occurred during my third trimester, and my identical twin was... strategically held back. You see, the Russian operatives—or as they say, the 'Vorovskoy Mir'—they had a very specific agenda. My twin was essentially put into what I can only describe as a natal holding pattern, a sort of... kompromat of the womb, if you will. The Bratva—that's the Russian criminal organization—they needed leverage, you understand. So my twin remained in utero as collateral until the ransom was paid. It was all very procedural, very... criminal enterprise.

jokes.text: Found them. They were on top of the microwave. — Wonderful. Oh, that reminds me. Can you get us some whitener for the coffees? — Oh... what have I been using?
jokes.setup: The ashes have been missing; a staff member announces they've been found on the microwave
jokes.punchline: The director immediately asks for coffee whitener, which prompts the staff member to realize what they've been using as creamer — the implication that the ashes have been used as coffee whitener
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's darkest and most structurally brilliant joke. The setup is pure misdirection — the ashes are found on the microwave (odd, but okay). Then the coffee whitener request, and the slow-dawning horror of 'Oh... what have I been using?' — delivered without the show ever making the implication explicit. The audience does the work. The restraint of not spelling it out is what makes it exceptional. It's earned (fully set up), original, and the economy of 'Oh... what have I been using?' is perfect. One of the best jokes in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, I know what Ted's secret is. He's in love with the farmer!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? No, he's not.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes he is! I can tell. They have this connection. It's like a romance novel waiting to happen.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No it's not! Picture it: Ted arrives at the farm at sunset, his tie slightly loosened. The farmer looks up from mending a fence, their eyes meet...

jokes.text: Well, I know this may sound premature, but have the two of you thought about the planning of your own funerals?
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are trying to leave the chaotic funeral home
jokes.punchline: The funeral director pivots to an upsell — pre-planning their own funerals — at the exact moment they're trying to escape
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy of the sales pitch at the worst possible moment. After the coffee whitener horror, the upsell is appropriately toned-down. Gets a recognition laugh — this is exactly what funeral directors do.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I was attacked by a goat.

jokes.text: David and Stevie's rapid exit — 'Okay, I think we got what we came here for, so we-I think we can just... thank you so much.'
jokes.setup: The funeral director has just asked them to pre-plan their own funerals immediately after the coffee whitener horror
jokes.punchline: David's stumbling, barely coherent escape attempt — 'so we-I think we can just' — is the verbal equivalent of a person backing out of a room
jokes.explanation: The cringe of escape. David's barely-constructed sentence as he tries to leave gracefully while profoundly unsettled is relatable and character-true. Not a standout moment but earns its place in the sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I was head-butted by a goat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that a sexual position?

jokes.text: Well, if I squint, I feel like I'm seeing your rough and rugged country cousin.
jokes.setup: Johnny has dressed up in Roland's clothes to appear working-class at the car lot
jokes.punchline: Moira's squinting assessment — 'rough and rugged country cousin' — describes what she sees while clearly indicating it's a stretch
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy is inferred: Johnny must look absurd in Roland's clothes. 'If I squint' does the work — Moira is trying very hard to see the desired effect. 'Rough and rugged country cousin' is a Moira-specific phrase for 'working class man.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We should scatter the ashes here in the parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: In the parking lot? Alexis, people park here. Someone's going to park on the ashes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So, that's disrespectful. We're not scattering ashes in a parking lot where they're going to be driven over by a 2007 Honda Civic.

jokes.text: Oh, and you feel confident you've hit the target with this?!
jokes.setup: Moira has assembled her own 'workaday woman' disguise after their earlier discussion
jokes.punchline: Johnny's incredulous question implies Moira's outfit is also a disaster — suggesting they're both equally ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy inferred from dialogue — we understand from Johnny's tone that Moira's 'dressed down' look is still extremely Moira. Sets up the car lot scene nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: My aunt had a nervous breakdown, lived in a car for three years, and now she works at a Applebee's in Reno. But hey, a girl could dream.

jokes.text: Oh, John, look. It's our car! — Well, Moira, let's not pick the first car we see. — No, before David was born! We bought a car just like this, don't you remember?
jokes.setup: They've arrived at the car lot; Moira immediately fixates on a car
jokes.punchline: When Johnny tries to be pragmatic, Moira reveals her reasoning — they had one like this before David was born — immediately elevating nostalgia over budget sense
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the car lot sequence. Moira's emotional rather than practical car-buying logic is consistent with her character. 'Before David was born' as the reference point for the last time they were car-buying is a nice Rose family detail.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, I think you're going to do great things. And when the time comes, I want you to know that I will personally ensure your ashes are scattered somewhere truly beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's... oddly sweet, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's the least I can do for a friend. Certainly better than that ditch behind the big box store.

jokes.text: Well, I don't remember the 'Everything Be Irie' bumper sticker.
jokes.setup: Moira insists this car is just like the one they bought before David was born
jokes.punchline: Johnny's only objection is the specific reggae-themed bumper sticker — suggesting everything else checks out
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Everything Be Irie' is perfect. Johnny's deadpan delivery suggests he's processed the whole thing except that detail. The fact that he DOES remember everything else is implicit, which is funny. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: Salesman's wife
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you're Dr. Clara Mandrake from 'The Crest of the Wave'!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm afraid you must have me confused with someone else. I don't watch television.

jokes.text: Yes, my poor 'usband, lost his job recently as a tennis pro, yes, at a public resort, that is, yeah.
jokes.setup: Moira is performing her 'workaday woman' character for the car salesman, claiming poverty
jokes.punchline: Her invented backstory — tennis pro at a public resort — is simultaneously too specific, slightly wrong-class (tennis pro is still aspirational), and delivered in an accent that keeps slipping
jokes.explanation: The 'tennis pro at a public resort' detail is deliciously wrong — Moira's conception of working-class is still too elevated. The dropped H on ''usband' signals she's doing a fake lower-class accent, and the trailing 'that is, yeah' is her improvising in real time. Very funny character performance.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to the Mercedes dealership.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Or we could just increase your meds.

jokes.text: It'll be nice to get off the streets, and be indoors for a change, yeah?
jokes.setup: Moira is building her poverty performance for the salesman
jokes.punchline: She implies they've been living on the streets — an escalation of the fictional backstory that's wildly overshooting the target
jokes.explanation: Moira's poverty performance escalates past 'struggling family' directly to 'homeless,' which is both an acting overshoot and accurate about where they could be headed. The deadpan 'yeah?' at the end suggests she's monitoring the salesman's reaction for signs it's working.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I can deliver the cat flea shampoo for you!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, that's very kind of you, but... there is no cat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I don't have a cat. I never had a cat. I was just buying flea shampoo... to have.

jokes.text: Moira... — What?!
jokes.setup: Moira has just told the salesman they've been living on the streets
jokes.punchline: Johnny says her name in pained warning; Moira responds with completely genuine confusion — she doesn't understand why he'd object
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum economy. The entire joke lives in the delivery: Johnny's pained 'Moira...' and her genuinely baffled 'What?!' The audience does all the work imagining Johnny's face. Classic sitcom beat executed perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, are you having a breakdown? Like, a mental health thing?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, Alexis, I'm 43.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, well that's still really young to be going through something.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm not going through anything. I'm just 43.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, but like, at 43, that's when men have those crises.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's not a crisis. It's just a number.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A number that means you're having a breakdown.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, 43 doesn't mean breakdown. It's just my age.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh! So it's like a code? Like 'code red'?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, Alexis, there is no code.

jokes.text: I'm from London, I was one of two identical twins. Tragically, I was snatched from my crib at birth by Russian mobsters.
jokes.setup: The salesman asks where they're from; Johnny has dodged; Moira takes over
jokes.punchline: Moira's invented origin story involves being kidnapped by Russian mobsters as an infant — offered with complete theatrical sincerity as part of her poverty narrative
jokes.explanation: This is a spectacular piece of Moira comedy. She's been asked a simple question, and instead of a simple lie, she constructs an entire soap opera. The kidnapping by Russian mobsters is a genuinely wild escalation — and it's delivered with the same sincerity she uses for everything. This is Moira in full performance mode, and it's one of the episode's highlights. High character integration — only Moira Rose improvises a Bratva kidnapping into a car negotiation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, 'cat' is code for hookup?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes, okay, fine. But I don't appreciate the crude terminology.

jokes.text: And what about the twin? — Well, if you're identical, I thought you'd both be valuable. — Yes, wouldn't you? — She wasn't born yet. — Yep, she wasn't born. — 'Til three minutes later, and the Bratva work very quickly.
jokes.setup: The salesman applies basic logic to Moira's twin kidnapping story
jokes.punchline: The twins couldn't both be taken because the other one wasn't born yet — three minutes later — and this explanation is offered straight-faced by both Moira and Johnny as if it resolves the logical problem
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's funniest extended bit. The salesman's perfectly reasonable question — if you're identical, the twin would also be valuable — is answered with complete straight-faced absurdist logic: she wasn't born yet. Three. Minutes. Later. And the Bratva work very quickly. The addition of 'Bratva' (proper mob terminology) as if this is a real and specific organization adds to the absurdity. The crowning detail is that both Moira AND Johnny collaborate on defending this insane story together, which tells you something about their marriage. The structure is flawless: setup (the hole in the logic), the implausible explanation, and the capper of 'very quickly.' One of the episode's best moments.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're Little Sexy Susan!

jokes.text: That is quite a story. — Isn't it? — Um, excuse me, I... I'll be back in a moment.
jokes.setup: The salesman has heard the full kidnapping story
jokes.punchline: His 'that is quite a story' is a masterpiece of restraint — and his immediate need to excuse himself to go process what just happened
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat lands well. 'That is quite a story' is the most restrained possible response, and the salesman needing a moment to himself is the audience's proxy reaction. Good button on the kidnapping sequence.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: To Stevie, for not following in her aunt's footsteps.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We're drinking to that?

jokes.text: I think he's buying it! — You think?
jokes.setup: The salesman has walked away looking shell-shocked from Moira's Russian kidnapping story
jokes.punchline: Moira reads his obvious disbelief as successful persuasion; Johnny's deadpan 'You think?' questions this interpretation
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. The entire joke is Moira's self-delusion and Johnny's weary skepticism in four words total. Their dynamic is captured precisely.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: According to these documents, you've inherited the entire motel.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What? The whole thing?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: The whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: ...I don't want the whole thing.

jokes.text: Lisa dropped them off. — Hey! Don't be embarrassed. I know your little secret, and as your assistant I will be discreet, and professional. — What? — No one's gonna know that you're having an adventurous little rendezvous with an apple-cheeked farm-woman!
jokes.setup: Alexis mentions Lisa dropped off something for Ted
jokes.punchline: Alexis has concluded Ted is having a secret romantic 'rendezvous' with a farm woman — her description ('apple-cheeked farm-woman') is both accurate and delightfully Alexis-coded
jokes.explanation: 'Apple-cheeked farm-woman' is a very Alexis description — slightly patrician, slightly romantic novel. The misdirection (we know the truth is a goat headbutt) makes her elaborate conspiracy theory funnier than it would be in isolation.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, she left me the motel? Are you sure she was of sound mind?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Like, actually, was she okay? Because that seems like something a person would only do if they had some kind of mental breakdown.

jokes.text: I was making a house call to Lisa's farm, and got... head-butted by her goat. — Is that when one of you is on the floor, and then the other one kind of just like hops... — No, uh... I was head-butted by an actual goat.
jokes.setup: Ted reveals his injury was from being head-butted by a goat — not a romantic incident
jokes.punchline: Alexis processes 'head-butted by a goat' and immediately tries to make it sexual again ('is that when one of you is on the floor...'); Ted has to clarify it was a literal goat
jokes.explanation: Alexis's mind goes somewhere entirely inappropriate with 'head-butted' and she describes what she's imagining with total sincerity. Ted's patient correction — 'an actual goat' — is perfect. The joke works because Alexis genuinely cannot default to the innocent reading. The reverse misdirection (we expected to stay in the romantic misunderstanding longer; instead we get Alexis making it MORE wrong) is strong structure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why do you both look like you're about to compete in a game show?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're going to look at cars.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We want to look serious and trustworthy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you look like you're one correct answer away from winning a jet ski.

jokes.text: Those are delicious, by the way. She practically begged me to try one. So... I did. And I also took a couple for David.
jokes.setup: Ted has revealed the goat-headbutt story and asked for discretion
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually admits she has been eating what we infer are goat-related farm products (possibly the same goat's cheese or produce), having helped herself without invitation, and then taken some for David
jokes.explanation: Alexis's casual acquisition and distribution of things that weren't offered to her is a character beat. 'She practically begged me to try one' when Lisa did no such thing, and then also taking some for David, is pure Alexis entitlement. Works as a closing button on the subplot.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We paid two hundred thousand quid for this car.

jokes.text: Um, yeah, you're right, this is a bit depressing. Are we sure this is the right place? — She was very specific about where she wanted to be scattered.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie arrive at the location where the aunt's ashes are to be scattered
jokes.punchline: The place is clearly bleak enough that David questions it — and Stevie confirms this is exactly where she wanted to be, which is a darkly perfect detail about the aunt's character
jokes.explanation: The aunt's specific, depressing choice of resting place says everything about who she was. David's 'a bit depressing' is an understatement, and Stevie confirming she was 'very specific' is the dark punchline. The aunt's character is established entirely through implication.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We paid what we had to pay for our dignity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you're wearing a caftan made of poolside towels.

jokes.text: Well, I don't want people parking on her! So... Maybe I could just put her on the grass over there?
jokes.setup: They're trying to figure out where exactly to scatter the ashes in this bleak location
jokes.punchline: Stevie's primary concern is that cars might park on the ashes — a consideration that is both practical and darkly absurd — and her solution (the grass 'over there') is offered with zero ceremony
jokes.explanation: The parking concern is perfectly Stevie — totally practical, completely unromantic. 'I don't want people parking on her' is a sentence that shouldn't be funny but absolutely is. The gesture toward 'the grass over there' as a ceremonial compromise is good deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That outfit is absolutely ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Wait, didn't you pick this out for me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would never.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You literally handed it to me this morning and said it was 'très chic.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, my taste has evolved since this morning.

jokes.text: Ooh, it smells like cigarettes. — Ohhh...
jokes.setup: David opens the urn/container of ashes
jokes.punchline: The ashes smell like cigarettes — confirming the aunt's lifestyle — and Stevie's 'Ohhhh' is an entire eulogy compressed into one sound
jokes.explanation: Two words and a groan — and it's devastating. The cigarette smell tells you everything about Aunt Maureen. David's simple observation, followed by Stevie's quiet 'Ohhh' of recognition, creates a micro-character portrait. It's also darkly funny because ashes smelling of cigarettes is a real thing, and the show leans into that truth. The economy is extraordinary.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I cannot imagine any scenario in which this would be an acceptable choice. Unless the dealership was actively on fire, and they had to grab the first car and drive away.

jokes.text: Um... do we scatter it all, or do we... save some for later? — Later?! — I don't know!
jokes.setup: They're about to scatter the ashes and Stevie has never done this before
jokes.punchline: 'Save some for later' — as if ashes are a snack — followed by Stevie's immediate defensive 'I don't know!' when called on it
jokes.explanation: 'Save some for later' is a wonderful dark absurdism from a character who has no framework for this situation. Stevie's genuine uncertainty and her defensive reaction when questioned is very true to her. The three-beat structure — question, incredulous response, defensive retraction — is clean.

jokes.text: This is gonna be me. — Well, theoretically, it's gonna be all of us. — Um, although I'm hoping that when I pass, there'll be a little more fanfare.
jokes.setup: Stevie looks at the bleak scene and imagines her own funeral
jokes.punchline: David agrees (death is universal) but then pivots to lamenting the lack of fanfare at this specific funeral — not reassuring, but self-referential
jokes.explanation: David's 'well, theoretically it's gonna be all of us' is a beautifully bleak consolation, and his immediate redirect to hoping for more fanfare at his own death is very David. The self-absorption within genuine empathy is his signature mode.

jokes.text: My great aunt worked at the motel. Front desk. — You never told me that. — Yep. So I just need to take up palm reading, get a few cats, move to Saskatchewan. Well, a girl could dream.
jokes.setup: Stevie sees her own future in her aunt's trajectory — motel worker, alone, bleak end
jokes.punchline: She maps out the additional steps needed to become her aunt — palm reading, cats, Saskatchewan — and then 'a girl could dream,' which treats this grim inevitability as an aspiration
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The escalating list of becoming her aunt — palm reading, cats, Saskatchewan — is funny on its own, but 'well, a girl could dream' is the devastating button. She ironizes her own despair, treating a grim future as a whimsical aspiration. This is Stevie at her most complex: genuinely scared but unable to be sincere about it. High character integration. The later reveal that she inherits the motel makes this funnier on rewatch.

jokes.text: And if they're not, I'll be sure to scatter your ashes in a much nicer parking lot.
jokes.setup: David has told Stevie her decisions will be great; the scene has been emotional
jokes.punchline: His promise to scatter her ashes in a 'nicer parking lot' is both a callback to their current situation and a darkly affectionate way of saying 'I'll be there'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's warmest and best-constructed joke. After an earnest pep talk, David wraps it in dark comedy — the callback to the parking concern from earlier, reframed as a promise of friendship. 'A much nicer parking lot' is both funny and genuinely touching. It's the exact register of the David-Stevie friendship: real affection wrapped in irony. A high-quality joke that works emotionally and comedically simultaneously.

jokes.text: Well, I hope you can forgive me, but... I couldn't help overhear your story. — Oh yes, love, it's an inspiration to many, I'm sure.
jokes.setup: The salesman returns with his wife, who overheard Moira's kidnapping story
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately accepts the compliment with gracious modesty — 'an inspiration to many' — about a completely fabricated story, without breaking character for a second
jokes.explanation: Moira's gracious acceptance of admiration for her fictional tragedy is very in character. 'An inspiration to many, I'm sure' is perfectly phrased — she's already imagining the wider audience for the kidnapping story. Character integration is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Sunrise Bay!' My wife was addicted to your show! — Well, I find it's wonderful, but we don't have a television, so.
jokes.setup: The salesman's wife has recognized Moira from her soap opera
jokes.punchline: Moira — caught being recognized as a celebrity while claiming to be a struggling working-class woman — denies having a television as her cover story
jokes.explanation: The cover story of 'we don't have a television' is simultaneously too convenient and very Moira — delivered with perfect composure in a crisis. The irony of a soap opera actress claiming not to own a TV is rich.

jokes.text: I remember when your twin showed up, and stabbed Armand with her sharpened crutch.
jokes.setup: The salesman's wife is describing the soap opera plot that Moira appeared in
jokes.punchline: The specific plot detail — a twin who stabbed someone with a sharpened crutch — is a parody of soap opera excess that also retroactively makes Moira's twin kidnapping story feel even more like a soap opera plot
jokes.explanation: The callback is the joke: Moira invented a twin kidnapping story for the salesman, and then the real soap opera she appeared in involved a twin with a crutch. The show has been following the same logic as Moira's improvisation — which is the deepest possible character joke.

jokes.text: Okay, look folks, I'm really sorry, my wife was simply... trying to fictionalize our very real financial struggle.
jokes.setup: Moira has been exposed as a celebrity despite their poverty performance
jokes.punchline: 'Trying to fictionalize our very real financial struggle' — Johnny's euphemism for 'lying' is both more elevated and more honest than he intends
jokes.explanation: 'Fictionalize our very real financial struggle' is a brilliantly constructed euphemism. It's accurate (they ARE struggling), ironic (she made up Russian mobsters), and elevated (the word 'fictionalize' dignifies what was outright fabrication). Johnny's delivery as a sincere explanation makes it land. One of the episode's sharpest lines.

jokes.text: Seeing you working so hard, just to shave a few bucks off that Lincoln... — You save that discount for someone who needs it!
jokes.setup: The salesman couple offers them a discount out of sympathy
jokes.punchline: Moira — who manufactured the entire poverty performance to get a discount — now refuses the discount on principle, calling it charity
jokes.explanation: This is a wonderful character reversal. Moira spent the whole scene performing poverty to get a discount, but when actual sympathy-based charity is offered, her pride reasserts itself. 'You save that discount for someone who needs it!' delivered with full Moira dignity is simultaneously hypocritical and admirable. It perfectly captures the Rose family's contradictions — performing poverty while being too proud to accept charity. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Um Moira, they're just trying to help us out by giving us a better price. — Well, nonsense, dear, we can certainly pay full freight. — Yes, okay. Dear, look. We really would appreciate any financial assistance.
jokes.setup: Moira has rejected the discount; Johnny tries to walk it back
jokes.punchline: Johnny's pivot from 'they're just trying to help' to flat-out 'we really would appreciate any financial assistance' when Moira doubles down is a complete reversal in one breath
jokes.explanation: Johnny's negotiation with Moira via the salesman — ending in his very direct 'we really would appreciate any financial assistance' — is a good escalation. The contrast between Moira's pride and Johnny's pragmatism driving a three-way negotiation is well-played.

jokes.text: Or perhaps we might have to take our business to a dealership that sells brand new cars. As you can see, we can't even afford her meds right now.
jokes.setup: The car deal is in flux; Johnny has just asked for help; Moira takes back over
jokes.punchline: Moira simultaneously threatens to go upmarket (brand new cars) AND deploys a new poverty detail (can't afford her meds) — the threats are completely contradictory and delivered without awareness of the contradiction
jokes.explanation: The double-play is beautifully constructed: Moira deploys both the rich threat (I'll go buy a NEW car) and the poor plea (can't afford meds) in consecutive sentences without noting the contradiction. It's Moira's most sophisticated negotiating tactic — she's playing both sides simultaneously. The 'as you can see' adds perfect confidence. High character integration.

jokes.text: Here we go, dear, here's the chair.
jokes.setup: The deal is done; the salesman goes to start paperwork
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately produces a chair for Moira — implying she was in 'I might faint' mode (or actually performing it) and he has learned to have a chair ready
jokes.explanation: The physical gag of Johnny immediately offering a chair implies Moira has been performing/experiencing faintness, and that Johnny has learned to anticipate this. The domesticity of the gesture within the absurd car lot negotiation is warm and funny.

jokes.text: Heather Warner doesn't actually have a cat.
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis have been discussing dropping off flea shampoo for a client's cat
jokes.punchline: Alexis drops this as a flat statement of fact — Heather has no cat, which means the whole flea shampoo errand is code for something else
jokes.explanation: This is a perfectly constructed reveal joke. Six words, no preamble, total deadpan. The entire context shifts in one sentence. Alexis has known this whole time and let the conversation go on anyway — possibly because she found it funnier that way. The economy is exceptional. The pause that presumably follows in the actual scene would be pure gold. One of the episode's strongest individual jokes.

jokes.text: Ohhh. — She like, thinks she has a cat? That's so sad. But I guess she is like 50, so that makes sense. — Yep, she's 42. And she's not crazy. It's... code.
jokes.setup: Alexis has revealed Heather doesn't have a cat; there's a beat of confusion
jokes.punchline: Alexis's immediate, sincere explanation (she thinks she has a cat, she's 50, sad) is so wrong in every detail (42, not crazy, it's code) that Ted has to provide a complete correction
jokes.explanation: Alexis's conclusion — she thinks she has a cat, she's 50, so that makes sense — is a character showcase. Every element of her explanation is wrong, and her ageism about 42-year-olds is casually appalling. Ted's patient three-part correction ('she's 42,' 'not crazy,' 'it's code') is perfectly structured. The escalation through Alexis's wrong answers is very funny.

jokes.text: I'm kind of seeing Heather, and the flea shampoo is... — A booty call?! — Well, I'm not crazy about the term, but...
jokes.setup: Ted begins to explain what the flea shampoo code means
jokes.punchline: Alexis completes his sentence with 'a booty call?!' — correct but jarring — and Ted's mortified semi-acceptance ('not crazy about the term, but...') implies she's technically right
jokes.explanation: Ted's elaborate code for something Alexis correctly identifies in two words creates good comic deflation. 'Not crazy about the term, but...' is a perfectly Ted response — politely accepting reality while maintaining dignity. The contrast between Ted's romantic sensibility and Alexis's blunt accuracy is excellent character work.

jokes.text: Ted, look at you, Little Sexy Susan! — Okay, calling me 'Susan' kinda takes the sexy out of it.
jokes.setup: Alexis is teasing Ted about his secret romantic situation
jokes.punchline: Her nickname 'Little Sexy Susan' is affectionate but undermined by the specific choice of 'Susan' — Ted points out the name destroys the compliment
jokes.explanation: 'Little Sexy Susan' is a great Alexis invention — enthusiastic and immediately self-sabotaging. Ted's precise diagnosis of why it doesn't work ('Susan kinda takes the sexy out of it') is good straight-man comedy. The specificity of 'Susan' as the most de-sexualizing name possible is well-chosen.

jokes.text: Ted, say hi to her cat for me. — That's too far, Alexis!
jokes.setup: Ted is leaving; Alexis calls after him
jokes.punchline: The callback to 'the cat' — now understood as a euphemism — as a sendoff, and Ted's exasperated 'That's too far, Alexis!' confirms it lands exactly as she intended
jokes.explanation: The callback earns a bonus for structure — the joke has been building through the whole subplot, and the euphemistic 'say hi to her cat' as a farewell is a clean button. Ted's 'That's too far, Alexis!' is the right reaction — exasperated, but the complaint validates that she got him. Good comedic economy.

jokes.text: To Maureen Budd, and not following too closely in her footsteps. — So we're drinking to me not becoming an alcoholic? — Mhmm. — Off to a good start.
jokes.setup: David proposes a toast at the ash-scattering ceremony
jokes.punchline: Stevie correctly interprets the toast as being about alcoholism; David confirms; Stevie notes they're already drinking, which is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Off to a good start' is the perfect button on what is a four-beat joke. The structure: toast about not becoming like the alcoholic aunt, Stevie correctly translates it, David confirms, Stevie notes the irony of drinking at the anti-alcoholism toast. The four beats are all tight and each earns its laugh. 'Off to a good start' as the punchline is deadpan perfection — Stevie's self-awareness about the irony delivered without any distress. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: When did you have this photo taken? — That's not me. — That's Maureen. — Mmm.
jokes.setup: A lawyer delivers Maureen's personal effects; David sees a photo
jokes.punchline: David has mistaken the aunt for Stevie — they look enough alike that David sees Stevie's future in a photo of Maureen. Stevie confirms this, and David's 'Mmm' is an entire verdict.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best-structured visual jokes. The setup: David sees a photo and compliments Stevie on it. The reveal: it's her dead aunt. David's 'Mmm' — a single syllable — is the entire punchline. The implications cascade: they look alike, Stevie's fears are validated, and David's 'Mmm' is both a polite retreat and an acknowledgment of what they're both thinking. The economy of 'Mmm' as the punchline is extraordinary. High character integration — this hits Stevie exactly where the episode has been building.

jokes.text: Okay, well, just because you bear a vague physical resemblance, does not mean that you... will end up in an apartment with newspaper for curtains.
jokes.setup: The photo has revealed Stevie looks like her deceased aunt; David tries to reassure her
jokes.punchline: His reassurance accidentally reveals new, specific details about the aunt's situation — 'newspaper for curtains' — that he apparently knows and that make things worse, not better
jokes.explanation: 'Newspaper for curtains' is a wonderfully specific and damning detail. David trying to reassure Stevie while accidentally introducing vivid new evidence of how bad the aunt's life was is excellent comedic structure. The reassurance makes things actively worse — a classic sitcom inversion.

jokes.text: Okay, this is not going to be you.
jokes.setup: There's presumably an additional devastating detail in the personal effects; David reacts
jokes.punchline: His insistence — 'this is not going to be you' — at this point in the scene has become a running correction as each new item makes the comparison more apt
jokes.explanation: Functions as a running-gag button — each reassurance becoming less convincing as more evidence arrives. The repetition of the denial pattern is itself the joke at this point.

jokes.text: What is this? — That is the deed to the motel. — Sorry? — Maureen Budd owned the motel. — You didn't tell me that. — Nobody told me that.
jokes.setup: A document in the personal effects reveals something unexpected
jokes.punchline: The aunt owned the motel — Stevie works at her dead aunt's motel without knowing it — and nobody told her, not even the aunt
jokes.explanation: The revelation lands as both a plot twist and a dark joke: Stevie has been working in her aunt's motel, following her aunt's footsteps, more closely than she knew. 'Nobody told me that' is delivered with stunned flatness. Sets up the 'was she of sound mind' escalation.

jokes.text: Okay, this still doesn't mean that you are going to be anything like her! I mean sure, on the surface, it is a step in that direction, but, you know, um, okay, you know what? Let's clear some of this stuff.
jokes.setup: Stevie has just been told she's inheriting the motel — following her aunt's path even more precisely than feared
jokes.punchline: David's reassurance collapses in real time: 'this still doesn't mean...I mean sure, on the surface, it is a step in that direction, but, you know...' — he trails into action, having failed to find an argument
jokes.explanation: The collapse of David's reassurance in real time is beautifully constructed. He starts confident, qualifies immediately ('I mean sure, on the surface'), qualifies the qualification ('it is a step in that direction'), and then completely gives up and pivots to busywork. The rhetorical surrender is very funny and very true to the situation — there's no good spin available.

jokes.text: You look like a contestant on 'The Price Is Right.' — Well, it worked, because the price was right.
jokes.setup: Johnny returns from the car lot wearing Roland's borrowed clothes
jokes.punchline: David's insult references the game show; Johnny's comeback repurposes 'The Price Is Right' as a literal description of a successful negotiation — a wordplay pun that also functions as a self-congratulatory button
jokes.explanation: A tightly constructed wordplay joke. David's 'Price Is Right' jab is a solid insult, but Johnny's instant comeback repurposes it as a compliment — the price WAS right. The pun is clean and the delivery as a button on the whole car-buying saga makes it land harder. It's also Johnny getting the last word for once, which is satisfying. One of the episode's most quotable exchanges.

jokes.text: How much did you pay for this? — David, it's not polite to ask what you paid for something. — It says $7,250 right there.
jokes.setup: David examines the car and wants to know the price
jokes.punchline: Moira's etiquette lesson about not asking prices is immediately undercut by the price being printed on the car
jokes.explanation: Moira's etiquette deflection is instantly demolished by the visible sticker price. The structure is clean: rule, immediate exception, David has already done exactly what the rule prohibited. 'It says $7,250 right there' delivered flatly is the perfect punchline.

jokes.text: Well, that's way over budget! — Well, that's not what we paid for it. — Well, what did you pay for it?! — $6,000 quid, love.
jokes.setup: The car cost $7,250 — well over the $4,000 budget
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals they actually paid $6,000, delivered with theatrical relief — but $6,000 is still $2,000 over budget; and 'quid' (British pounds) implies she doesn't know what currency she's using
jokes.explanation: '$6,000 quid, love' — the mixing of dollars and British pounds slang, delivered as if this is good news, is perfect Moira. The word 'quid' when the currency should be dollars suggests she's so deep in character she's lost track. Or simply that she doesn't distinguish. Both readings are funny. The escalation from 'over budget' to 'more over budget than expected' is well-constructed.

jokes.text: And it would've been a lot less, if your mother hadn't talked them back up! — You can't put a price on dignity.
jokes.setup: Johnny reveals that after getting the price down, Moira insisted on paying more
jokes.punchline: Moira's counter — 'You can't put a price on dignity' — is the same logic she used to reject the charity discount, now deployed to justify voluntarily paying more
jokes.explanation: 'You can't put a price on dignity' is probably the episode's most quotable single line. The irony is maximal: after an entire episode of Moira pretending to be poor, performing poverty, and manufacturing elaborate backstories to get a better deal, she ultimately paid MORE than necessary because accepting charity offended her dignity. The line crystallizes her entire worldview and the episode's comic logic in six words. Excellent character integration — this is the distillation of who Moira Rose is.

jokes.text: Uh, tell that to your outfit. — This is your outfit. — What?!
jokes.setup: Moira has invoked dignity; David calls her out via her ridiculous costume
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals David's costume critique is aimed at clothes he chose and bought — David's 'What?!' is a double-take of someone realizing they've been outwitted
jokes.explanation: The callback to David buying Moira's dress is satisfying here — Moira turns the table with 'this is your outfit' and the implication that criticizing it is self-criticism. The three-beat rhythm — criticism, reveal, reaction — is clean and the 'What?!' lands as a strong button.

jokes.text: Look, the important thing is, we now have a car! — Is this a car for dead people? — No, it's not a hearse!
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to end the argument by celebrating the car purchase
jokes.punchline: David's 'is this a car for dead people?' implies the car looks like a hearse — and the accusation is so specific and apparently apt that Johnny's denial sounds defensive
jokes.explanation: The hearse accusation is good — buying a Lincoln that reads as a funeral vehicle is a perfect visual gag for this episode about funerals and death. The timing of 'is this for dead people' immediately after a scene with actual ashes is a nice structural coincidence/callback.

jokes.text: Were all the other cars on fire, then?
jokes.setup: Johnny says the car isn't perfect, it's a used car
jokes.punchline: David's question implies the only way this car could be the best available choice is if all alternatives were on fire
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's sharpest closing lines. The logic is airtight and devastating: the only scenario in which this car is the optimal choice is if every other car was destroyed. The specificity of 'on fire' (not just gone, but specifically on fire) adds physical comedy to the image. Seven words, maximum contempt, perfect economy. David Rose at his most withering. High quotability — this is the kind of line you repeat immediately after watching.

jokes.text: It's very big.
jokes.setup: The family is looking at/inspecting their new car
jokes.punchline: Moira's final verdict on the car — 'It's very big' — delivered as if this is a neutral observation but implying it's her only positive she could find
jokes.explanation: Three words as the episode's final beat (or near-final). Moira's diplomatic understatement — finding exactly one thing to say about the car and choosing its size as the most neutral possible observation — is a good closer. The economy is perfect. The implication (this is the best I can do) is very Moira.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira try to get a deal on a used car while David helps Stevie put her great aunt to rest.
insight: Character comedy drives 86-joke territory as Rose's car obsession spirals into pure absurdism.
